prior.title: Getting to Zero: Green Corridors
prior.summary: Directory of 62+ established green shipping corridors globally.
prior.what_is_it: Global directory of 62+ established green shipping corridors across major trade routes.
prior.why_matters: Green corridor directory shows which trade routes have zero-emission vessel options or are developing them — directly affects routing decisions for event logistics.
prior.reasoning: Comprehensive directory of active green shipping corridors — directly affects lane options.
prior.full_brief:
# Green Corridors — Getting to Zero Coalition
## Regulatory Fact Document | MONITORING

---

# 1. Purpose and Scope of This Document

This brief covers the Getting to Zero Coalition's green corridors initiative as a forward-intelligence subject: an industry-led decarbonisation mechanism operating at the intersection of voluntary shipping-sector coordination, emerging binding IMO regulation, and country-level e-fuel production policy. It synthesises the primary source (Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum) with corroborating sources covering the Brazil–Belgium corridor launch, IMO MEPC 83/84 regulatory developments, and WEF analysis of e-fuel scaling conditions.

The brief is structured as a Market Signal Brief, reflecting item type `initiative`. The green corridors initiative is not itself a binding regulation; it is a voluntary multi-stakeholder programme that is now being institutionally backstopped by the IMO Net-Zero Framework adopted at MEPC 83 (April 2025). Where the IMO Framework intersects, relevant regulatory obligations are flagged.

**Conventions used throughout:**
- FACT claims are cited inline with verbatim source spans.
- ANALYSIS claims open with one of the four closed-set label tokens.
- *Legal Confirmation Required:* flags items requiring counsel review.
- The workspace is referenced by operational profile, never by name.
- All dates are as of the time of generation (June 2026).

**Re-check window:** The IMO Net-Zero Framework adoption vote is scheduled for MEPC 85, November 2026. Set a monitor for that date — it converts the signal from voluntary to binding for the workspace's ocean-mode operations.

---

# 2. What's Moving and What Triggered It — MONITORING

**The initiative and its current scale**

The Getting to Zero Coalition — managed by the Global Maritime Forum and co-founded with the World Economic Forum and Friends of Ocean Action — was launched in September 2019 with 70 signatories. FACT: "more than 200 organisations (including over 180 private companies) within the maritime, energy, infrastructure, and finance sectors" are now members, covering maritime, energy, infrastructure, and finance. *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum, [undated, current as of June 2026]. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition.

FACT: The Coalition is "committed to getting commercially viable zero-emission vessels powered by zero-emission fuels into operation by 2030." *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition.

**Green corridors growth and the feasibility wall**

FACT: As of November 2024, "Green corridors grow by 40% worldwide, but face a 'feasibility wall', says Global Maritime Forum report." *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition news headline, Global Maritime Forum, 19 November 2024. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition.

FACT: A September 2025 headline from the Coalition's own news feed states: "Shipping off track to meet 5% zero-emission fuel target by 2030 as new report issues 'serious wakeup call'." *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum, 25 September 2024. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition.

Analytical inference: The 40% growth in corridor count combined with the "feasibility wall" framing indicates that proliferation of corridors has outpaced the commercial-scale fuel supply needed to run them — the constraint has shifted from political coordination to production economics and offtake financing. This is the primary operational risk for workspaces whose ocean-mode clients are relying on corridor-linked green fuel claims for Scope 3 reporting.

**What triggered the current phase**

FACT: The IMO reached a net-zero agreement in April 2025: "IMO reaches agreement but more needed to unlock future fuels," per the Coalition's news feed dated 11 April 2025. *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition.

FACT: The SAFETY4SEA corroborating source reports, as of 4 June 2026, a "New consortium to advance e-fuel green corridor between Brazil and Belgium," with consortium partners including HIF Global, NYK Line, Höegh Autoliners, and Wallenius Wilhelmsen — all of whom are listed as Coalition member companies. *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition news item, Global Maritime Forum, 4 June 2026. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition.

Analytical inference: The Brazil–Belgium corridor represents the Coalition moving from feasibility study to structured consortium formation — a phase transition that typically precedes offtake agreements and infrastructure financing decisions by 18–36 months, based on comparable green-corridor development timelines (Rotterdam–Singapore corridor as a reference case).

---

# 3. Who's Driving It and What They Want

**Coalition governance and membership**

FACT: "The Coalition is managed by the Global Maritime Forum, which initially founded it together with the World Economic Forum and Friends of Ocean Action." *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition.

FACT: "Membership in the Coalition's 'race to the top' for the future of our industry is by invitation only." *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition.

**Named member organisations with direct relevance to ocean freight forwarding and cargo principals**

From the member list, the following categories are relevant to workspaces operating as freight forwarders, NVOCCs, or cargo principals on covered trade lanes:

- *Ocean carriers present:* A.P. Moller – Maersk, Hapag-Lloyd, MSC Mediterranean Shipping Company, ONE, HMM, Yang Ming, ZIM Integrated Shipping Services, Wan Hai Lines, X-press Feeders, Unifeeder, NYK Line, Mitsui O.S.K. Lines, K Line, DFDS, Pacific Basin, Swire Shipping, Wallenius Wilhelmsen, Höegh Autoliners. FACT: All are named in the member list. *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition.
- *Logistics and forwarding companies present:* CEVA Logistics, DHL Global Forwarding, DSV, Kuehne+Nagel, Agility. FACT: All named in the member list. *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition.
- *Cargo principals present:* BHP, Rio Tinto, Tata Steel, Unilever, BMW Group, Volvo Cars, Bunge, COFCO International, Cargill, Trafigura. FACT: All named in the member list. *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition.
- *Ports present:* Port of Rotterdam, Port of Antwerp-Bruges, Port of Hamburg, Port of Açu, Vancouver Fraser Port Authority, Port of Seattle, Port of Montreal, Port of Gothenburg, Port of London Authority, Port of Aarhus, among others. FACT: All named. *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition.

**What the named parties want**

Analytical inference: Carrier members (Maersk, Hapag-Lloyd, MSC, ONE) use corridor participation to pre-position for the IMO Net-Zero Framework's fuel-intensity reduction pathway — which, once formally adopted, will affect their operating economics directly. Early corridor investment provides optionality on future-fuel sourcing before commercial-scale supply is locked up by long-term offtake deals.

Analytical inference: Logistics forwarder members (DHL Global Forwarding, Kuehne+Nagel, DSV) participate to develop "green lane" product offerings that respond to Scope 3 reporting pressure from shipper clients under CSRD and ISSB S2, enabling premium freight products differentiated by verified low-emission routing.

Analytical inference: Cargo principal members (BHP, Rio Tinto, Unilever, BMW Group) participate to secure priority access to green freight capacity on commodity-heavy routes (iron ore, automotive, consumer goods) before green capacity becomes scarce, and to meet downstream supplier-sustainability requirements being imposed by buyers.

**Supporting organisations that set policy direction**

FACT: Supporting organisations include the International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), the Energy Transitions Commission, UNCTAD, Environmental Defense Fund, and Smart Freight Centre. *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition.

---

# 4. Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**Regulatory backstop: IMO Net-Zero Framework**

FACT: The IMO press release (corroborating source) states MEPC 83 approved the Net-Zero Framework. *Source: IMO press release on MEPC 83, IMO, [April 2025]. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/imo-approves-netzero-regulations.aspx.

FACT: The EDF corroborating source (May 2026) describes ongoing ISWG-GHG 22/23 intersessional work and tracks the regulatory timeline toward a MEPC 85 November 2026 adoption vote. *Source: EDF, May 2026. https://www.edf.org/media/imo-continues-work-net-zero-framework-path-global-action-shipping-decarbonization.

FACT: The Coalition's own news feed notes, dated 6 May 2026: "Negotiations signal progress but uncertainty remains on the Net-Zero Framework." *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum, 6 May 2026. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition.

**Key conversion trigger:** MEPC 85 is the vote where the IMO Net-Zero Framework moves from approved-in-principle to formally adopted and enforceable. Analytical inference: If adopted at MEPC 85 (November 2026), the Framework creates binding fuel-intensity and GHG-levy obligations on vessel operators — converting corridor participation from voluntary market differentiation into a compliance-linked commercial necessity for carriers on covered routes.

**China, India, Brazil corridor expansion**

FACT: The Coalition's news headline dated 27 November 2025 reads: "China, India, and Brazil move on green shipping corridors as industry awaits climate regulations." *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum, 27 November 2025. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition.

FACT: The S&P Global corroborating source (November 2025) cites the GMF Annual Progress Report and covers China/India/Brazil corridor expansion alongside the "feasibility wall" cost-gap challenge. *Source: S&P Global Market Intelligence, November 2025. https://www.spglobal.com/market-intelligence/en/news-insights/research/2025/11/series-of-new-green-shipping-corridors-emerge-despite-imo-regulatory-setback.

FACT: The Coalition's news feed dated 30 October 2025 states: "Study shows South Africa-Europe shipping route could run on green ammonia by 2029." *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum, 30 October 2025. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition.

FACT: The Brazil–Belgium (Açu–Antwerp) corridor consortium launch was reported 4 June 2026, naming Port of Açu and Port of Antwerp-Bruges among the coalition members involved. *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum, 4 June 2026. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition.

**E-fuel scaling and the COP30 Belém context**

The WEF corroborating source (January 2026) frames the Açu–Antwerp corridor as a potential first large-scale e-fuel export route and contextualises cost barriers and a COP30 Belém pledge. *Source: WEF, January 2026. https://www.weforum.org/stories/2026/01/location-cost-policy-clean-fuels-hydrocarbons/.

Analytical inference: COP30, scheduled for Belém, Brazil in November 2025, created political momentum specifically for Brazilian e-fuel export corridors. The Açu–Antwerp consortium launch in June 2026 suggests the political signal has converted into structured commercial investigation — but not yet into signed offtake agreements or financing commitments, which remain the outstanding conversion step.

**Feasibility wall — the binding constraint**

Analytical inference: The "feasibility wall" label (GMF, November 2024) describes the gap between corridor political commitments and commercially viable fuel supply at scale. The critical unknown is whether green fuel production costs fall fast enough for corridors to become economically self-sustaining before the IMO Net-Zero Framework imposes a carbon levy that shifts the economics by regulatory fiat rather than market pull.

---

# 5. Operational and Cost Implications If It Materialises

*No content for this section as of June 2026: specific green-fuel surcharge rates or premium cost figures for corridor-linked freight are not available from primary sources in the source blocks provided. Carrier-specific green-freight premiums require direct carrier data requests; current rates are not publicly available from the sources provided.*

**Directional cost framing (labeled):**

Analytical inference: Workspaces in the freight-forwarding role that move cargo on ocean lanes covered by active corridors (Rotterdam–Singapore, South Africa–Europe, Brazil–Europe) will face an emerging bifurcation: standard ocean freight priced at conventional fuel economics, and green-corridor freight carrying a green-fuel premium. The premium magnitude is a research gap; the direction is upward and the timeline compresses as IMO Framework adoption approaches.

Analytical inference: For workspaces whose ocean-mode cargo moves automotive parts, consumer goods, or high-value commodities on transatlantic or Asia-Europe lanes — routes where Coalition carrier members (Maersk, Hapag-Lloyd, MSC, ONE, Wallenius Wilhelmsen) are the dominant capacity providers — the green-fuel premium will first appear as a voluntary green-lane surcharge, then potentially as a regulated levy pass-through once the IMO Framework is adopted.

Analytical inference: Workspaces acting as freight forwarder for cargo principals that are themselves Coalition members (Unilever, BMW Group, Volvo Cars, Cargill) may face client-driven green-routing requirements in advance of regulatory mandates, because those principals have made public commitments to the Coalition's Ambition Statement.

---

# 6. Competitive Implications

**Who is positioned inside the coalition**

FACT: Major logistics and forwarding competitors DHL Global Forwarding, Kuehne+Nagel, DSV, CEVA Logistics, and Agility are named Coalition members. *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition.

Analytical inference: Coalition membership provides early access to green-corridor offtake discussions, carrier working groups, and policy advocacy positions before these channels are open to non-members. For workspaces not currently in these networks, the competitive gap is informational and relational rather than technological at this stage — but that gap converts to a commercial disadvantage when carrier green-capacity is allocated first to coalition-linked forwarders.

Analytical inference: Workspaces without Coalition membership or equivalent corridor-intelligence sourcing are operating one reporting cycle behind on green-route availability, green-fuel surcharge trajectories, and carrier decarbonisation commitments — all of which are increasingly appearing in RFQ evaluation criteria from large cargo principals under CSRD Scope 3 disclosure pressure.

**Named competitor access status (sourced from member list)**

- DHL Global Forwarding: Coalition member. Green corridor intelligence: direct access. *Source: Member list, Getting to Zero Coalition. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition.
- Kuehne+Nagel: Coalition member. Same. *Source: ibid.*
- DSV: Coalition member. Same. *Source: ibid.*
- CEVA Logistics: Coalition member. Same. *Source: ibid.*
- Agility: Coalition member. Same. *Source: ibid.*

**Competitive edge available to the workspace — COMPETITIVE EDGE**

Analytical inference: The window to develop credible green-corridor product knowledge — before the MEPC 85 November 2026 adoption vote converts corridor participation from voluntary to compliance-linked — is approximately five months. Workspaces that develop corridor-specific routing analysis and carrier relationship intelligence in this window can credibly respond to RFQs from Coalition cargo-principal members before competitors have formalised their green-product documentation.

---

# 7. Client Conversation Talking Points — What the Workspace Can Credibly Say

**What is safe to assert (grounded in sourced facts)**

- "The Getting to Zero Coalition now includes more than 200 organisations — including the major ocean carriers your cargo moves on — committed to zero-emission vessels by 2030." *(FACT, sourced.)*
- "Green corridors grew 40% in 2024, but the Global Maritime Forum's own reporting identifies a 'feasibility wall' — the number of corridors has outrun commercially available zero-emission fuel supply." *(FACT, sourced.)*
- "The IMO adopted a Net-Zero Framework at MEPC 83 in April 2025. A formal adoption vote is expected at MEPC 85 in November 2026. That vote is the regulatory moment that converts corridor investment from voluntary to compliance-linked." *(FACT, sourced from corroborating sources.)*
- "A new consortium — including NYK Line, Höegh Autoliners, and Wallenius Wilhelmsen — announced in June 2026 that it is investigating a green e-fuel corridor between Brazil's Port of Açu and Antwerp. This is the active edge of corridor development." *(FACT, sourced.)*
- "Your logistics providers — including DHL Global Forwarding, Kuehne+Nagel, DSV, and CEVA Logistics — are Coalition members and are already in the working groups where green-capacity allocation decisions will be made."

**What to avoid saying (pitfalls)**

- Do not represent that any named corridor is currently operational with zero-emission fuel at commercial scale — the sourced record shows corridors are in feasibility, assessment, or early consortium stages.
- Do not quote green-fuel surcharge figures — no sourced rate is available from the source blocks provided.
- Do not represent that the IMO Net-Zero Framework is formally in force — the adoption vote is still pending as of June 2026.
- Do not describe the workspace as a Coalition member unless that membership is confirmed independently.

**Questions to pose in client meetings**

- "Does your Scope 3 reporting framework currently distinguish between conventional and green-lane ocean freight? If not, when does it need to?"
- "Which of your ocean carrier contracts include green-fuel optionality clauses, and who controls the trigger?"
- "If a green-fuel surcharge appears on your transatlantic or Asia-Europe shipments in 2027, is your budget structured to absorb it or pass it through?"

---

# 8. What the Workspace Should Do Now

**Action 1 — Map cargo exposure to active corridor routes** | MONITORING
The workspace, in its freight-forwarding role, should identify what share of its ocean-mode volume moves on the five most active corridor development routes: Rotterdam–Singapore, South Africa–Europe, Brazil–Europe (Açu–Antwerp), West Australia–East Asia, and China corridor routes. FACT: All five are referenced in the Coalition's news reporting as active corridor development sites. *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition. This mapping produces the exposure picture needed to price green-routing optionality into carrier negotiations before MEPC 85.

**Action 2 — Brief carrier account teams on MEPC 85 timing** | WINDOW CLOSING
Analytical inference: The five-month window before MEPC 85 (November 2026) is the optimal moment to open corridor-linked green-capacity discussions with carrier account managers at Maersk, Hapag-Lloyd, MSC, ONE, and Wallenius Wilhelmsen — all Coalition members — before post-adoption demand for green-lane allocation spikes. Waiting until after adoption puts the workspace in a reactive queue behind cargo principals that have been in Coalition working groups for years.

**Action 3 — Add green-fuel surcharge contingency to commercial proposals** | COST ALERT
Analytical inference: Client proposals for ocean freight on active corridor lanes should include a conditional clause covering potential green-fuel premium pass-through, triggered by IMO Framework adoption or by carrier imposition of a green-lane surcharge. The specific surcharge rate is not publicly available from the sources provided; legal and commercial teams should confirm the appropriate contingency mechanism before the next RFQ cycle.

**Action 4 — Track the Coalition's Annual Progress Report** | MONITORING
FACT: The Coalition released its Annual Progress Report on 7 May 2025 ("Getting to Zero Coalition report highlights members' progress and systemic bottlenecks") and appears to issue an annual update each November–December (November 2024 report on 40% growth). *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition. Set a re-check for November 2026, which will likely carry the first post-MEPC 83 progress assessment and may confirm or revise the feasibility-wall assessment.

**Action 5 — Evaluate Smart Freight Centre relationship** | COMPETITIVE EDGE
FACT: Smart Freight Centre is a named supporting organisation of the Coalition. *Source: Getting to Zero Coalition, Global Maritime Forum. https://globalmaritimeforum.org/getting-to-zero-coalition. Smart Freight Centre maintains the GLEC Framework for freight emissions accounting, which is the methodology most commonly accepted for Scope 3 Category 4 reporting under CSRD and ISSB S2. Engaging with Smart Freight Centre's working groups provides corridor-level emissions intensity data before it is publicly available.

---

prior.intersection_summary: Overlaps with [Resolution MEPC.377(80) – 2023 IMO GHG Strategy] on the binding GHG reduction targets that green corridors are designed to precede; green corridor fuel cost premiums are a commercial precursor to the levy mechanism the Strategy will formalise. Overlaps with [EEXI and CII FAQ] and [MARPOL Annex VI] on vessel operator CII obligations that drive carrier operational changes (slow-steaming, surcharges) affecting forwarder booking costs and transit times on the workspace's Americas, Europe, and Asia ocean lanes. The IMO Net-Zero Framework reward mechanism — currently unresolved as of October 2025 — will determine whether green corridor fuel premiums are offset by policy incentives or passed through to freight rates in full.
